lens.included_activities: US manufacturers of stock or custom nonupholstered wood household furniture and freestanding cabinets in the lower-middle-market band that provide recurring or repeat outsourced production, private-label, retailer, dealer, designer, hospitality, replacement, or support work to external customers.
lens.excluded_activities: Kitchen cabinets and fixed countertops, upholstered furniture, furniture repair or refinishing, nonwood furniture, one-off craft work without recurring or repeat customers, captive units, shells, and non-control financing vehicles.
lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Customers include retailers, dealers, designers, private-label brands, hospitality buyers, distributors, and households. Revenue comes from stock collections or custom orders and can include repeat replenishment, private-label programs, finish or size options, replacement parts, warranty work, and after-sale support.

a.bridge: BLS reports occupations and wages for broader NAICS 337100 household and institutional furniture and kitchen cabinet manufacturing, not wage-weighted task exposure for eligible NAICS 337122 firms. I mapped management, product design, merchandising, sales, purchasing, quoting, order processing, planning, customer support, inspection, and CNC programming to partial AI exposure while assigning little exposure to cutting, machining, sanding, assembly, finishing, packing, and handling. The proxy may overstate exposure for craft-heavy shops or understate it for configurable private-label and direct-order businesses.
a.rationale: The broader comparison group reported 66.03% of employment in production occupations. AI can assist visualization, configuration, bills of materials, merchandising, forecasting, procurement, order entry, scheduling, support, and quality documentation, while most woodworking and finishing remain physical. Existing CNC automation also means some machine tasks are not current unautomated opportunity.
a.change_evidence: A wage-weighted task inventory from payroll and time-study data at representative NAICS 337122 firms, split among design, sales, office, plant, logistics, and warranty functions, would materially change this estimate.
caveats: The occupation source is broader than the six-digit industry and dated 2016.
The mapping estimates unautomated task exposure rather than observed AI use and does not separate stock, custom, and ready-to-assemble production.

rho.rationale: Visualization, configuration, demand planning, procurement, order processing, customer communication, and documentation can adopt AI with moderate change. Automated inspection, yield optimization, production scheduling, and design-to-CNC integration require accurate catalogs, machine connectivity, vision systems, exception handling, cybersecurity, and human quality control.
rho.change_evidence: A survey of eligible manufacturers documenting live AI use cases, implementation cost, verified labor hours, material yield, scrap, rework, forecast accuracy, and warranty outcomes would materially move this estimate.
caveats: DOE evidence establishes general manufacturing feasibility rather than industry adoption rates.
Legacy systems, variable wood inputs, finishing quality, product-safety testing, and small-firm digital maturity are not measured directly.

e.rationale: Retailer replenishment, private-label production, dealer programs, designer relationships, and hospitality orders can qualify as recurring or repeat outsourced work. Branded product-only firms, direct one-time custom shops, owner-dependent craft businesses, captive plants, and firms outside the target earnings band are excluded.
e.change_evidence: A firm census linking NAICS 337122 companies to normalized EBITDA, repeat-order share, channel mix, private-label revenue, customer concentration, owner dependence, service scope, and ownership would determine eligibility.
caveats: The Census scope includes stock, custom, assembled, and knockdown furniture but does not measure recurring relationships or transferability.
The assigned firm count uses a machinery margin bridge that may not fit wood-furniture profitability.

s5.rationale: Private wood-furniture manufacturers plausibly face succession and consolidation pressure, but no six-digit owner-age or completed-control-transfer series was found. Strategic acquisitions and family succession can qualify; internal reorganizations, brand-only deals, and closures without transferable operations do not.
s5.change_evidence: A five-year transaction census from PitchBook, GF Data, Axial, BizBuySell, and furniture-sector advisers matched to eligible firms, combined with owner-age and succession surveys, would materially change the probability.
caveats: This is bounded judgment without observed industry-specific transfer rates.
Small private transactions, asset sales, and plant purchases are inconsistently disclosed.

q.rationale: Design, finish, customization, delivery reliability, short domestic lead times, and safety or emissions compliance can preserve part of productivity gains. Retailer bargaining, private-label agreements, promotions, comparable products, input-cost transparency, imports, returns, and warranty obligations pass substantial value through.
q.change_evidence: Retailer and private-label contracts, price lists, promotional allowances, rebate terms, bid histories, compliance costs, return and warranty agreements, and realized margins after productivity changes would materially change retention assumptions.
caveats: No source directly measures retention of AI-enabled productivity benefits.
Retention is likely higher in custom or differentiated domestic programs than in standardized knockdown furniture.

d5.bridge: BLS projects real output for the broader furniture and related products sector to grow at 1.0% annually through 2034, not constant-quality demand for eligible US nonupholstered wood-furniture manufacturers. I converted the annual rate into a five-year ratio but lowered the base and widened the interval for housing turnover, discretionary purchases, retailer inventory cycles, and domestic-versus-import share. The proxy may overstate this industry if cabinets or other categories lead growth, or understate it if wood furniture and custom domestic production outperform.
d5.rationale: Nonupholstered wood furniture remains a physical household durable, but purchases can be deferred and depend on household formation, moving, credit, and retailer inventories. Broad furniture output is projected to grow modestly, while June 2026 housing data show continued completions alongside softer permits. Product-safety and composite-wood rules sustain compliant replacement supply but do not guarantee volume.
d5.change_evidence: Product-level domestic shipment units, retail sell-through, import volumes, inventories, household formation, home sales, replacement cycles, and constant-quality forecasts for wood bedroom, dining, living-room, storage, and ready-to-assemble furniture would materially tighten this ratio.
caveats: The output projection covers the entire furniture sector rather than NAICS 337122.
Housing units and safety rules are drivers or constraints rather than direct quantity measures.
Retail inventory corrections and imported ready-to-assemble furniture can cause domestic production to diverge from final demand.

o.rationale: Year-five demand remains for physical furniture that must be designed, sourced, machined, assembled or packaged, finished, tested where applicable, labeled, delivered, and warranted. Software can enable visualization, configuration, and direct ordering but cannot replace the product. Imports, flat-pack supply, and retailer-controlled sourcing can displace a US manufacturer from part of retained demand.
o.change_evidence: Retailer sourcing data, import penetration by wood-furniture class, ready-to-assemble share, customer willingness to pay for domestic customization, and direct-to-consumer fulfillment evidence would materially change this estimate.
caveats: The estimate applies after the demand change in d5 and avoids double counting cyclical volume loss.
Physical demand can persist while accountable supply shifts from US lens firms to imported products or retailer-owned specifications.

narrative.executive_view: Eligible firms are wood household-furniture manufacturers with repeat retailer, private-label, dealer, designer, or hospitality programs. AI can improve configuration, design, planning, ordering, support, and inspection, but woodworking and finishing remain physical. Persistent product demand is tempered by discretionary timing, imports, ready-to-assemble substitution, and concentrated channels.
narrative.ai_impact: AI can accelerate visualization, product configuration, bills of materials, merchandising, demand planning, procurement, order entry, scheduling, customer support, and quality documentation. Cutting, machining, sanding, assembly, finishing, packing, and material handling remain physical, and advanced plant use cases require reliable data and integration.
narrative.value_capture: Design, finish, customization, domestic lead times, reliability, and compliant products can retain value. Retailer and private-label customers can demand lower prices, allowances, or service improvements, while promotions, comparable imports, returns, and raw-material visibility increase pass-through.
narrative.firm_availability: The assigned firm population is estimated using a machinery margin bridge that may not fit wood furniture. Eligibility depends on target earnings, recurring or repeat external-customer programs, transferable operations, low owner dependence, and manageable customer concentration.
narrative.demand_durability: Households continue to need physical storage, dining, bedroom, and living-room furniture, and safety or composite-wood rules preserve accountable supply. Purchases are discretionary and tied to moves, housing, credit, and retailer inventories. Broad output is projected to rise modestly, but domestic manufacturers can lose share to imports and flat-pack models.
narrative.risks_and_uncertainty: Key risks are imports, retailer concentration, promotions, freight, lumber and panel costs, housing cycles, inventory corrections, fashion obsolescence, product tip-over liability, formaldehyde compliance, finishing constraints, and owner dependence. Firm eligibility, domestic demand share, and transfer rates are not observed directly.
narrative.principal_driver: Repeat retailer and private-label programs create recurring physical demand with AI-addressable configuration, planning, ordering, and service workflows.
narrative.principal_weakness: Physical production, discretionary purchases, imports, flat-pack substitution, and channel power constrain both implementation and value retention.

sources.title: Census Bureau Profile: 337122 Nonupholstered Wood Household Furniture Manufacturing
sources.supports: Official scope covering stock or custom, assembled or knockdown nonupholstered wood household furniture and freestanding cabinets.

sources.supports: Broader furniture and cabinet occupation mix, including the production-occupation share used for the AI-exposure bridge.

sources.supports: Manufacturing AI applications including automated visual quality control, operations optimization, and predictive maintenance.

sources.supports: Employment and real-output projection for the broader furniture and related product manufacturing sector.

sources.title: CPSC Adopts Mandatory Safety Standard for Clothing Storage Units
sources.supports: Mandatory stability testing and safety requirements for dressers, armoires, wardrobes, and other clothing storage units.

sources.title: Formaldehyde Emission Standards for Composite Wood Products
sources.supports: TSCA Title VI testing, labeling, recordkeeping, import-certification, and third-party certification requirements for composite wood and finished goods.

sources.title: Monthly New Residential Construction, June 2026
sources.supports: Current housing permits, starts, and completions used as context for household-furniture demand and housing-cycle uncertainty.
